text.title: Retro-ADR — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that make Retro-ADRs honest, useful, and politically safe to author.

rule.statement: Retro-ADR authoring is explicitly no-blame. The template MUST NOT contain fields like `author_responsible` or `reason_undocumented`; the practice records what was decided, not who failed to write it down.

rationale.source: Westrum organisational typology; Allspaw blameless postmortem culture

rule.rationale: Retro-ADR exists because forward-only-ADR culture failed in this team. Adding blame to retro capture reintroduces the same disincentive. The only acceptable framing is We have shipped code and we are now writing down what shipped.

rule.statement: Every retroactive ADR carries `status: accepted-retroactively` (or equivalent) AND a `decided_on_estimated` field with an explicit precision (`week-of-2026-03`, `q1-2026`, `unknown`). Forward-looking ADRs keep `accepted` / `proposed` etc.

rationale.source: MADR status field practice; archival best practice

rule.rationale: Mixing retro and forward ADRs without a status marker corrupts the history. A future reader cannot tell which decisions were considered before code vs reconstructed after. The explicit status preserves epistemic honesty without de-valuing retro records.

rule.statement: The Retro-ADR body MUST separate `original_intent` (what we now believe the team meant at the time) from `current_reconstruction` (what we today understand the consequences are). Each field cites its evidence sources.
rule.rationale: Without the split, retro-ADR collapses two different epistemic acts (recovery vs interpretation) into one prose. Readers cannot tell which parts are recoverable history vs current opinion. The split makes the boundary visible.

rule.statement: Retro-ADR capture runs on a recurring weekly cadence (60-90 minutes) and captures ≤3 ADRs per session. Batch-capturing 20 in a single offsite is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Marathon capture sessions produce shallow ADRs. A 90-minute ritual targeting 1-3 decisions yields enough depth per ADR. Weekly cadence keeps the backlog short enough to remain accurate.

rule.statement: Each Retro-ADR has exactly one named owner who signs off. Multi-owner Retro-ADRs are forbidden because they encode disagreement as consensus.
rule.rationale: When two authors co-sign a retro-ADR they disagree on, the document smooths over the disagreement and becomes unreliable. Single ownership forces the document to reflect one specific reconstruction; alternative readings are written as separate ADRs or in the `alternatives_considered_now` field.

example.title: Retro-ADR header showing the three honesty fields
example.code:
--- id: ADR-0042 title: Authentication uses opaque tokens, not JWT status: accepted-retroactively decided_on_estimated: q3-2025 authored_on: 2026-05-20 owner: jonas@co sources: - PR #312 - chat thread #auth 2025-09-12 - commit 7f3a9b2 ---
## Original intent We chose opaque tokens because the auth flow already terminated at our gateway and JWT validation would have required a sidecar per service. Source: PR #312 review comments.
## Current reconstruction Opaque tokens shifted the validation cost to a Redis lookup on every request. This is fine at our scale (≤200 RPS) but will need revisiting if we shard horizontally. Source: load-test report 2026-04.
## Alternatives considered now JWT with short TTL + JWKS would have removed the Redis dependency. Cost: every service needs JWK refresh logic. Not adopting until horizontal sharding pressure.
example.note: Illustrates r2 (status), r3 (intent vs reconstruction split), r5 (single owner).

example.title: Blame-leak retro-ADR
example.code: status: accepted-retroactively why_we_never_wrote_it: Alex left before documenting notes: this should have been written 6 months ago
example.note: r1 violation (names absent author, frames as failure). The document is technically correct but politically poisonous; nobody will author the next retro-ADR.
